intro:
The Church, in biblical usage, refers to the assembly of those called out by God — a meaning reflected in the Greek ekklēsia, from which the concept derives. The word appears in two senses in the New Testament: the universal body of all believers united to Christ as head (Ephesians 5:23–25), and the local congregation gathered in a particular place. Paul's letters address specific local churches while simultaneously teaching that all believers form one body across time and geography.

The Church's origin lies in Christ's gathering of disciples and the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost. Easton notes that three Greek words are used in Acts 19 — the regular civic assembly (ekklēsia), the irregular mob, and the lawful assembly — illustrating how the early Christians adapted a common civic term for their covenant community. The Church's defining marks are the preaching of the Word, the administration of baptism and the Lord's Supper, and the exercise of discipline.
